summary: Evans shares her excitement about reading Martin Kleppmann's 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' and explains the concept of log-structured storage she learned from it. She breaks down two fundamental approaches to database storage: log-structured engines (which append writes sequentially for faster writes) and page-oriented engines like B-trees (which update data in place). She illustrates the concept with a two-line bash script that functions as the 'world's simplest database.' She also clarifies the distinction between log-structured storage and write-ahead logs, explaining how WALs protect against data loss during crashes.
key_points: Database storage fundamentally divides into two approaches: log-structured (append-only, faster writes) and page-oriented (B-trees, faster reads)
A functional database can be built with just two bash functions that append to and grep a text file, illustrating the core idea of log-structured storage
Write-ahead logs are distinct from log-structured storage — both types of storage engines can use WALs to prevent data loss on crash
The tradeoff between LSM and B-tree storage shows up clearly in benchmarks: LSM has higher write throughput, B-tree has higher read throughput
The word 'consistency' has at least five completely unrelated meanings in database contexts, and ACID's 'C' is about application-level invariants, not linearizability
money_quotes: I seriously thought atomicity in databases was the same idea as atomicity in concurrent programs like an 'atomic instruction' and it's a TOTALLY DIFFERENT THING.
It's really helpful to know that there are actually 5 completely unrelated uses of the word 'consistency' when talking about databases.

Basically this appends to a text file (called `database`) every time you write, and greps that text file for the latest update every time you read. I tried it! This is a totally functional database :)
The idea here is that this way you don't need to make random disk writes – you can just write to the end, and so writes can be a lot faster.
Writing to disk is expensive. Because of that, databases will often just do writes to memory, and then save their state to disk later. This is smart and efficient!
But if you crash, then you can lose data. Losing data is bad. To avoid losing data, you can just log all your writes to disk by appending to a file.
key_insight: Database storage engines split into two fundamental approaches — log-structured (fast sequential writes) and page-oriented (fast random reads) — and understanding this distinction unlocks comprehension of real-world database design tradeoffs.
